title: if / elif / else
subtitle: Teach your programs to make decisions — and finish Week 1 like a champ!
goal: Use if, elif, and else to choose what your code does, master the colon and 4-space indentation, and combine conditions with and/or/not.

blocks.heading: Programs that choose 🚦
blocks.html:
So far your programs run every line, no matter what. Today they get superpowers: they can decide. Just like a traffic light says green = go and red = stop, Python can say “if this is true, do that.”

This is one of the most important ideas in all of coding. Take your time — you'll use it forever! 🎉

blocks.heading: The if statement
blocks.html:
An if checks whether something is True. If it is, Python runs the lines inside. If not, it skips them.

Two things are brand new here, so read carefully:

  • The line ends with a colon :
  • The lines inside are pushed in with 4 spaces — this is called indentation.

blocks.caption: Your first decision
blocks.code:
score = 85

if score >= 60:
    print("You passed! 🎉")

print("This line always runs.")
blocks.explain: Because score (85) is >= 60, the indented line runs. Notice the colon after the if line, and the 4 spaces before print. The last line has NO spaces, so it always runs.

blocks.html: Indentation = which lines belong together. The 4 spaces tell Python “these lines are inside the if.” Lines that line up on the far left are NOT inside it. Most editors add the 4 spaces for you when you press Enter after a colon.
Watch out! Forgetting the colon : or messing up the spaces is the #1 mistake beginners make. If Python says IndentationError or SyntaxError, check your colon and your 4 spaces first. 🔍

blocks.heading: else: the otherwise case
blocks.html: What if the test is False? Add an else for the “otherwise, do this” path. Exactly one of the two blocks runs — never both.

blocks.caption: Can you ride the rollercoaster? 🎢
blocks.explain: height_cm is 120, which is NOT >= 130, so the if is False and the else block runs instead. Try changing 120 to 140 in your head — which line would print then?

blocks.heading: elif: more than two choices
blocks.html: Sometimes there are lots of paths, not just two. elif (short for “else if”) lets you check more conditions in order. Python checks them top to bottom and runs the first one that's True, then stops.

blocks.caption: Turn a score into a grade 🏅
blocks.explain: Python tries each test from the top. 78 is not >= 90, not >= 80, but it IS >= 70, so it prints Grade: C and skips the rest. Order matters — biggest checks usually go first!

blocks.heading: Even or odd? Meet % (the remainder)
blocks.html: The % sign (called modulo) gives the remainder after dividing. A number is even when dividing by 2 leaves a remainder of 0.

blocks.caption: Even or odd checker
blocks.explain: number % 2 finds the remainder after dividing by 2. For 7 the remainder is 1, not 0, so it's odd. Remember: == means 'is equal to' (two equals signs!).

blocks.heading: Combining conditions with and / or / not 🧠
blocks.html: Remember Day 6? You can join tests together! Use and when both must be true, or when at least one must be true, and not to flip true and false.

blocks.caption: Two rules at once
blocks.explain: The if is True only when BOTH parts are true: age is at least 8 AND has_ticket is True. Since 10 >= 8 and the ticket is True, you get into the movie!

blocks.heading: Putting an if inside an if (nesting) 🪆
blocks.html: You can place an if inside another if. The inner one just gets more indentation — 8 spaces instead of 4. We call this nesting, like Russian dolls.

blocks.caption: A nested check
blocks.explain: First Python checks is_weekend. Only if that's True does it look at the weather. Count the spaces: the inner if uses 8, because it lives inside the outer if's 4.

blocks.question: What goes at the END of an if line, right before the indented code?
blocks.explain: Every if, elif, and else line ends with a colon :. The colon says 'an indented block comes next.'

blocks.question: How many spaces do we use to indent the code inside an if?
blocks.explain: Python's standard is 4 spaces. Being consistent is what matters — 4 spaces is the friendly default everyone uses.
80 is not >= 90, but it IS >= 80, so the first matching branch runs and prints B. Python stops checking after the first True.

blocks.title: Hot or cold? 🌡️
blocks.instructions: There's a variable temp set to 30. Write an if/else: if temp is 25 or higher, print "It's hot!" — otherwise print "It's cool!". Don't forget the colon and 4 spaces!
blocks.starter:
temp = 30

# write your if / else below
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your program made a real decision! 🔥
blocks.failMsg: Use 'if temp >= 25:' then an indented print, then an 'else:' with its own indented print. Check your colons and 4 spaces.

blocks.title: Big, small, or just right
blocks.instructions: The variable num is 7. Use if / elif / else to print "Big" if num is greater than 10, "Small" if num is less than 5, otherwise print "Just right". Make sure all three branches are there!
blocks.starter:
num = 7

# write if / elif / else below
blocks.passMsg: Three branches, one winner — you nailed elif! 🏅
blocks.failMsg: You need 'if', then 'elif', then 'else', each ending in a colon with a 4-space indented print below it.

challenge.title: Build a theme-park gatekeeper 🎢
challenge.instructions: You're the ride operator! There are two variables: height_cm (set to 140) and age (set to 9). Decide who can ride: if the rider is at least 130 cm tall AND at least 8 years old, print "Enjoy the ride! 🎉". If they are tall enough but too young, print "Tall enough, but too young.". Otherwise print "Sorry, not this time.". Use and to combine the two rules, and use elif for the middle case.
challenge.starter:
height_cm = 140
age = 9

# write your if / elif / else gatekeeper below
challenge.hint: Start with the strictest rule first: 'if height_cm >= 130 and age >= 8:'. The elif only needs to check the height, because if you reached it the age must have been too low.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Incredible! You just built real decision-making logic — and that's a wrap on Week 1. You can now make programs that think. See you in Week 2! 🚀
